questions.description: Kildare County Council purchased land in 1965. Adverse Possession.
questions.text: Kildare County Council purchased land in 1965. It was originally intended to use the land for a housing development at some stage in the future. In and around 2001, Jones began to use the land to graze his horses. In 2006, Jones built some makeshift fences to corral the horses, and he provided a trough for the horses to drink from. In 2007, Jones installed a gate at the entrance to the property to which he has the only key. The Council is now about to sell the land to the Department of Education and it is planned to build a school on the site. Jones has just initiated proceedings claiming adverse possession of the land. Advise Kildare County Council.

questions.description: Answer [both]{.underline} parts (a) Concisely explain the similarities and… Easement, Director.
questions.text: Answer [both]{.underline} parts (a) Concisely explain the similarities and differences between the characteristics of an easement and a profit-ä-prendre. and (b) Neil bought a house located on a half-acre site in 1998. The land next to the site belonged to Claire who hardly ever visited it. Claire told Neil that she intended to build a house there someday. In 2006, Neil started crossing Claire's land to gain access to a road along which buses going into the local town stopped. In 2017, Neil learned that Claire had sold her field to Anlex Limited which plans to build a large warehouse and depot on the field. Around that time, the managing director of Anlex Limited told Neil that once this building is complete, maximum security will be applied and there will be no question of Neil taking short cuts across the field. Neil is also concerned that the large warehouse and depot to be built by Anlex Limited may impede the flow of light to his greenhouse. Advise Neil.

questions.description: The Residential Tenancies Act 2004 contains a number of significant measures for dealing with the private rented sector…
questions.text: The Residential Tenancies Act 2004 contains a number of significant measures for dealing with the private rented sector in Ireland, in particular, in the area of security of tenure and tenancy termination procedures. Consider whether the Residential Tenancies Act 2004 has succeeded in its aim of providing a clear legislative framework in respect of tenancy obligations for landlords and tenants in Ireland.

questions.description: Jim dies on the 1 st April 2017, at the age of 75 years leaving a wife,… Succession.
questions.text: Jim dies on the 1 st April 2017, at the age of 75 years leaving a wife, Samantha, and their two children, Molly, aged 16, a schoolgirl, and Mandy, aged 26, an electrical engineer; as well as Samantha's child from a previous relationship, Leanne, aged 32, whom Jim adopted when she was a baby. Jim paid for Leanne's University education, but she has had problems with alcohol since she was young and dropped out of college after a couple of years. About a week prior to Jim's death, Molly was involved in a car accident which left both her legs badly damaged, and she now has limited mobility. Mandy is married. Prior to her wedding she had planned on purchasing a house with her husband. Her father had found out about this and said to her "Sure why would you do that? This house will be yours and Molly's after your mother's time." (Jim was referring to the family home which was solely owned by him). Mandy never went through with the purchase of the house, but Jim never spoke of the matter again. In Jim's will, Samantha is left a token sum of €1 ,000 and nothing else. He leaves all of the residue of the estate, including his house, to Molly. Advise the parties on the legal issues that are likely to arise in relation to succession to Jim's estate.

questions.description: Outline and discuss [ONE]{.underline} of the following: (i) The nature and operation of Judgment mortgages.
questions.text: Outline and discuss [ONE]{.underline} of the following: (i) The nature and operation of Judgment mortgages. or (ii) The scope and importance of the Supreme Court judgment in Webb v. Ireland [1988] IR 353. or (iii) The meaning and legal significance of the term "business equity" within the context of the Landlord and Tenant Acts 1980-1994.

questions.description: Fred and Gina Murphy are a married couple who live with their two children… Mortgage.
questions.text: Fred and Gina Murphy are a married couple who live with their two children in a small house in Dublin. The house is solely owned by Fred; however they have lived there together throughout the ten years of their marriage. Fred runs his own business organising corporate outings. The business is not going well, and so to raise extra money, he decides to take out a second mortgage on the house. He does not tell his wife about this and induces her to sign the necessary documentation by telling her it is "just routine bank stuff'. Fred normally takes care of all of the family's financial affairs, so Gina does as he asks. Fred's business goes bust and he defaults on his loan. The bank wants to sell the house to recover the money. Advise Gina.

questions.description: Outline and discuss the means by which a joint tenancy may be converted into a tenancy in common, both in law and…
questions.text: Outline and discuss the means by which a joint tenancy may be converted into a tenancy in common, both in law and equity. Your answer should take account of the changes, if any, introduced by the Land and Conveyancing Act 2009.

questions.description: Answer [BOTH]{.underline} parts (a) Concisely set down the legal requirements that must be followed to execute a valid…
questions.text: Answer [BOTH]{.underline} parts (a) Concisely set down the legal requirements that must be followed to execute a valid will, as well as any other technical matters that must be borne in mind when drafting such a will so as to ensure the testator's intentions are given full effect. and (b) Outline and assess the Irish Courts' approach to the admissibility of extrinsic evidence in the construction of wills.

questions.description: Discuss the essential characteristics of an easement and explain the ways in which an easement may be acquired.
questions.text: Discuss the essential characteristics of an easement and explain the ways in which an easement may be acquired. Use relevant legal authority to support your answer.

questions.description: In 1988, Fran inherited a small cottage from her uncle. Advise Jack.
questions.text: In 1988, Fran inherited a small cottage from her uncle. She visited the property on a couple of occasions but decided that she did not wish to live there as it was much too quiet and isolated in her job and friends were located in the city. Nonetheless, she decided to keep the house as somewhere to go for a break at weekends, a holiday home in the summer and ultimately a retirement home. She asked the neighbour next door -- Charles -- to 'keep an eye' on the cottage for her and gave him a spare key in the event an emergency arose. However, work became very busy for Fran that year, the result of which was that she never had the chance to stay in the cottage. A couple of years later, Charles began using the shed at the rear of the cottage to store his bicycle and his garden tools. In 1994, he decided to carry out some repairs on the shed as well as the grounds which had become quite dilapidated and in a state of disrepair. This work, which included a new gate and padlock (for which he had the only key) cost him €500. Since Fran had not been in contact with Charles, he never told her about this. A year later, Charles started to use the house to accommodate friends who came to visit. He also started to rent it out during the summer months and kept the money himself. In 2013, Charles sold his land to Jack and handed over the keys to Fran's house. He informed Jack that Fran had not been around for years. Jack continued to use Fran's property as if it was his own. In early 2018, Fran returned to her cottage and demanded her keys back. Advise Jack.

questions.description: Outline and assess the approach of the Irish Courts to the admissibility of extrinsic evidence in the construction of…
questions.text: Outline and assess the approach of the Irish Courts to the admissibility of extrinsic evidence in the construction of wills.

questions.description: In 2014, four close college friends, David, Paul, Matthew and Alex, bought… Advise the parties.
questions.text: In 2014, four close college friends, David, Paul, Matthew and Alex, bought a duplex apartment together. Title to the apartment was not registered in the Land Registry. They each paid an equal share of the purchase price. Shortly afterwards, David lost his job and decided to emigrate and sold his interest to Paul. In 2015, Paul unexpectedly became a father and decided, without legal advice, that it was a good time to make a will whereby he would leave all of his share in the property to his son. In 2016, Matthew moved out and rented a room in a house a few doors away because he was finding it very difficult to study for his final year exams in the apartment due to elevated noise levels. This caused an argument between Alex and Paul which resulted in Paul removing all of Alex's possessions, including his plasma screen television and his X Box, from the living room and locking the living room door. On hearing of this, Alex's girlfriend suggested that he live with her in her house and so Alex moved out. Matthew and Alex believe that Paul should pay them rent. Alternatively, Matthew suggests that the apartment should be sold. Alex is uncertain as to whether the apartment should be sold because its market value still is not quite what it was when they purchased it. Paul says that he is not willing to pay them rent and that they are welcome to stay in the apartment. He objects to selling the apartment because he will not be able to find another home in the vicinity that is suitable for him given its close proximity to his place of work. He is also concerned as to whether his son would be adequately provided for if he were to die unexpectedly. Advise the parties.

questions.description: Analyse and discuss the law concerning freehold covenants as it is set out under the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform…
questions.text: Analyse and discuss the law concerning freehold covenants as it is set out under the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009.

questions.description: When Olive discovered that she had a terminal illness in January 2018, she… Advise Nicola.
questions.text: When Olive discovered that she had a terminal illness in January 2018, she decided to draft a will. At the time, she was undergoing an aggressive form of treatment and so she didn't have the energy to go to her solicitor. She asked he civil partner of four years, Nicola, to download a free will template from a website she had heard about. Olive invited her close neighbours, Tom and Jerry, over for dinner that evening with her and Nicola. Over a glass of wine after dinner, Olive began filling in a number of versions of her will. Once she was happy with the contents (€150,000 to her partner Nicola, €20,000 to her niece, Julie and the remainder to the local animal rescue service), she asked Tom and Jerry if they could sign their names to the document. When Tom and Jerry left, Olive began tidying up. When disposing of the many versions of the will she had drafted, she accidentally threw the proper version in the bin. The next morning, when Nicola realised Olive's mistakes, she cello-taped the will back together and put in the drawer. In June 2018, Olive sadly passed away. Nicola recently brought the will to a local solicitor's office where she has been met with questions concerning the content and validity of the will. Advise Nicola.

questions.description: Write a note on any [two]{.underline} of the following three topics.
questions.text: Write a note on any [two]{.underline} of the following three topics. Refer, where appropriate, to relevant case law: (a) Bare licences (b) Proprietary Estoppel (c) Contractual licences

questions.description: Harry and Sally got married in 2012. Mortgage.
questions.text: Harry and Sally got married in 2012. They moved into their new home in Athlone which was located on a 100-acre farm in the countryside. Both the family home and the farm were in Harry's name. In 2015, Sally discovered that Harry was having an extra-marital affair with her best friend and so she moved back in with her parents in Cork. Harry distracted himself with the farm work and decided that an expansion of the business would be a good idea. He applied to take out a mortgage with the bank. When the bank asked if he was married, Harry replied 'My wife is dead to me.' The bank took this literally and suggested that Harry would have to sign the statutory declaration to say that his wife was deceased in order to proceed with his application. Harry secured the mortgage. However, over time, the reality of the marriage breakdown hit Harry and he lost all interest in everything including the farm. This resulted in him defaulting on his mortgage repayments from 2016. Sally recently became aware through Harry's sister that the Bank now intends to sell the farm including the family home. Sally is furious and claims she never consented to this mortgage on the property. Advise Sally.
